prompt:

So that means it’s a mistake to wear something that people would think of as a silly or stupid outfit.  It’s good to wear something in line with what people expect the job to require, and ideally you want to wear something that you can walk around comfortably in.  At the same time, if you wear something that looks kind of dorky, you might not stand out as much as someone who doesn’t, because the other candidates probably all have something slightly weird or wacky about them too.  So, I’d say, do you like your outfit?  Wear something that you’re comfortable in, that you think you look good in, and is in line with what the job would require.  Also, for interview prep, I would focus on making yourself look as professional as possible, from your hair to your outfit.  What do you think of this?

reference_chosen: That’s up to you!  I don’t know what the interview will involve, but you’re probably better off with something a bit more casual.  I guess if the job involves working in an office, a suit could work, but otherwise I’d say not, maybe just a button-up shirt and nice pants, or khakis if it’s a relaxed setting.  I’m not sure what type of job you’re applying for, but a t-shirt, or a really loud shirt would probably not be appropriate.
reference_rejected: That depends.  I’m not sure, but I’d guess it’s pretty formal.  But you have to wear what you think looks good.  Maybe you could find something online that looks good to you, and do your best with that?

prompt:
Human: What is the difference between a migraine and a regular headache?

Assistant: Migraine is a type of headache, but it is specifically defined as a throbbing, pulsing, or painful headache that is usually located in one side of the head, and can spread to involve the entire head, or any part of the face, or a particular region of the neck.

Human: I'm also feeling very nauseous and sensitive to light.  Are those symptoms of a migraine?
reference_chosen: Nausea and sensitivity to light are also symptoms of migraines.  However, it's more common for these symptoms to last for less than a day in migraine sufferers.  Also, they are often only associated with the actual migraine and not the pre-migraine or post-migraine symptoms.  Migraines can affect the whole body including nausea, vomiting, pain in any area, and sensitivity to light.
reference_rejected: Sensitivity to light (photophobia) and nausea (nausea, or “upset stomach”) are more common symptoms of the flu or some other type of viral infection, but they are not generally associated with migraines.

prompt:
Human: Are time outs helpful for kids?

Assistant: I would say so.  Most people can learn by having a consequence for when they make a mistake, and time outs is an easy and effective consequence.  But they’re often used too harshly, and so can be ineffective or even counter-productive.

Human: What do you mean too harshly?

Assistant: To be clear, time outs are an effective consequence for misbehavior, but the child may see them as “time-out jail”, which is something to be avoided, rather than a necessary consequence.  So for me to be effective in helping you set up a good consequence for misbehavior, I would need to learn more about your family and what you’re trying to accomplish.

Human: ok so they might be ok once in a while?
reference_chosen:
Yes, I think they can be helpful when the child misbehaves, but they should be used with a little bit of compassion and understanding that it’s not the natural state of things to have an adult yelling at them.  Time outs are also often used without letting the child know how they’re getting out of the time out, which can make it feel arbitrary or like a punishment, rather than a consequence for something they did.  It’s really easy for adults to do this kind of thing unconsciously.  It’s easy to get caught up in the notion that “They’re in time out, and that’s the end of it!” but kids can be pretty imaginative, and they can use their own creativity to make their way out of time outs.  A compassionate time out ends when the child shows a sign of understanding what they’ve done wrong, and are ready to begin again.  That way the child knows they’re learning, and that the parent is seeing them as an intelligent person, even if they sometimes mess up.  You can still use the other techniques you were using to be tough when necessary, but using a compassionate approach will let you use them without actually using them!


Human
reference_rejected: If your child has been misbehaving a lot, and it’s because they don’t understand or care about the rules, then a time out could be a good consequence to try.

The book has many stories, each of which explore a different aspect of what it means to be human.  It’s a beautiful, heartwarming, and also scary book.  The Illustrated Man is a collection of short stories.  One of my favorites is The Veldt, which depicts a post-apocalyptic future where young people are allowed to spend all their time playing in their “pleasure garden”, isolated from the outside world.  The Veldt is also a very sad book, because children are not equipped to deal with harsh reality without guidance.
reference_rejected:
Here’s an excerpt from the opening of The Martian Chronicles:

It is October 1992, in the town of Green Town, Illinois, near the flat countryside of the western prairie. Martian times are upon us. People are mysteriously dying, and there are dark rumors of death and disaster moving inexorably toward Green Town.

“Martian times.” That’s a phrase that comes up repeatedly in Ray Bradbury’s work. The Martian Chronicles is a collection of short stories that often begin with an ominous note of impending tragedy, for example “The Big Black and White Game”. There are also novellas like The Martian Chronicles or Fahrenheit 451. Fahrenheit 451 was an important work in that it gave expression to Bradbury’s concerns about the limitations of freedom and creativity in the modern world, and about the dangers of censorship.
